Hey, it's Sean and Andre. We are doing our week four review for the Momentan Builder workshop in addition. This week we're going to be reviewing a series of relationship building emails. We just discussed before this. We have no idea where this is going. We're looking at three emails, thousands of words. We've read through them several times and we're just going to jump in and see where it leads. And my plan really is just to put all the pressure on Andre and I'll just ask questions along the way but I haven't told them yet. So it may be something about that is wrong. Yeah, so I think one of the one of the nice things with having so many people submit their work is that each time we do a review, we get to pick something completely different. So there were elements that we thought was were interesting with Cohen's, you know, with the work that he's creating for this for this first part of the email series. That's why we chose this one. So as a whole, I think it's, you know, bearing in mind, I think it's worth just drawing attention to the fact that everyone's work is their first drafts, is their first shitty drafts. That was the whole idea behind this workshop. It's not about creating something perfect. It's about creating your shitty first drafts. And, you know, once this course is over, you get to do around two, three, four until it's at a point where you feel it's good to go and expose it to the realities of Facebook. So there were some nice ideas inside this one. I've just got a few notes here that the like from a matter idea. So zoomed out, it was conversational, felt that what it was story driven. He's got a nice use of images. There's at least one image in each email. So I'm just scrolling down quickly. Yeah, so there's an image in every email. And the images tie nicely with the narrative of that email. And it can help paint a picture around the story that's been written in a way that you don't have to keep drawing attention to certain things. And then I also like the fact that he's got his picture, his avatar, his, you know, a picture of his face on his signature file when he's signing off. And, you know, for a series that's meant to build a relationship with somebody that's a complete stranger, being able to see the person that's writing this email to you, I think, is something that's lacking in many cases. You know, it's not a requirement. It doesn't have to be there. But I think it definitely does help. I can't tell you how many websites I've been to. And one of the first places I go to when I visit a website, because it's that thing where you can try and see who's behind this thing as I go to the About page. And there's so many About pages that there's no image of the person, of the company, of the people behind it. There's like zero. And it's written almost like a stock image type of thing. It's just, you know, this is a company established from blah, blah, blah, and we do this and that. And that's it. I hate it when I come across things like that. So the fact that that Cohen's got his face there, you can see who he is. It's got it, you know, in his signature. There's a summary of what he does. He's a life coach, relationship coach and trainer. So, you know, it's nice and clear. It's there. And it's kind of out of the way as well. So I think that's my initial 30,000 foot view of the three emails. Becca, juice, Sean. It was sort of flowed naturally. There, parts of it for me are disjointed. But again, we've got to be careful with this because in my heart of hearts, I'm an editor. So it's hard for me to just read and not edit and read at the same time. And that's, that's not how an audience is going to read it. So I have to be cautious about this. So the fact that it's there and it exists and it's well done, that's, that's incredible. And now it just gets, it's a matter of polishing and, and rather than polish at the, you know, at the sentence level, and, which is probably not very helpful for everybody listening to this call, it's, I think it's better if, certainly from my perspective, if I were hearing this, I would want to know sort of broadly speaking, are there any, any editing themes that to pay attention to? There are a few in here. And this may very well be, you know, Cohen, your, if your native language is Dutch and you're writing in English, first of all, crazy respect for that, because I'm able to write in precisely one language. And that, that in and of itself is a stretch for me. So some of this may be the Dutch, English distinction. It may just be a different in the way languages use differently. But there are, as I was reading it, I found myself wondering about some things. Let me see if I can find an example here. So like this question, or this, the sentence, but what about the helping process itself? I don't know what the helping process is. I have no idea what that means. And that happens a lot in writing. We all do this. And we'll refer to this or that or we'll use phrases like that, but we don't, we haven't actually explained what it is. And because we're constantly filling in information as the writer, we know these things. And we're, we can assume that the reader knows them too. And we shouldn't do that. And what makes it even more difficult when I was an editor in a magazine, one of the rules we had is what we couldn't read a piece more than three times from an editing perspective. Because by the time the third time we had read something, our brain was filling in many of the details that weren't actually on the page. And so you can't see it anymore for what it is. And that becomes very difficult when we're editing our own work. It's one of the reasons to have somebody else edit your work is helpful or to look at your work. Because we might say things that make perfect sense to us because we filled in all of the context, yet the person reading it does not have that context. I'm trying to find where I said there were many examples of this. I can't, of course, I can't find one. So I'm going to hand it off to you for a second. Alrighty. For an example. So right at the top of email one, and I think this is this is broadly useful. Although I am highlighting something that Cohen's done specifically on the page here. It's something that we can all be mindful of, I think, or try and use. So right at the very top, and he's written, he's called this, you know, because the fact that this whole thing is about using the nature and the outdoors as a canvas to do coaching and connect with people. He's written here. Keep in mind that the benefit frame is always there when entering the green arena. And he's got the green arena in single quotes, and he's capitalized green and he's capitalized arena. So that that seemed important because it's been capitalized. So it's a subconscious signal to the reader that pay attention to this thing because drawing attention to it of capitalized, you know, green and arena, the first characters of it. Yet it's never ever used again, which seems like a terrible waste. I think, I mean, when I first read this on the first read through, I thought, ah, he's now establishing this idea that of the forest and nature as the green arena, which I thought was really smart, but then it was never used again. So I think that's an oversight or mistake. I think he could totally leverage that throughout because now he's, he's he's kind of, you know, given it a frame and somebody reading that is going to understand what the green arena is now. And it's something that he can attach to himself now. Like, you know, this is his trademark term for this thing that means going outdoors and connecting with people and coaching and using, you know, trees and wildlife as metaphors to help coach. So I think that's something that I think on his, on his rewrites, he can maybe work that in. So yeah, that's that. Do you have, have you found your thing or should I continue? Yeah, yeah, I found a couple of examples. And again, just to be clear, this may be, this may be a translation. So bear, keep that in mind. So here, this very first email, here are a few different sentences. Today's email is about how Peter Kreiger discovered a way to integrate nature, integrate nature with what? Like that to me, that sentence ends without, I don't, I don't know what it means. And then tomorrow we will go into the powerful elements that nature provides for the helping process. I don't know what the helping process is, provides to whom. And then in the last email was trying to light on synchronicities in nature, but impact the results, what results. We have to, we have to make it very clear to the reader that we say Peter discovered a way to integrate nature. I know from having read this several times that he's, he's integrating nature into a therapeutic process, into the sort of the overall therapeutic process. We have to state that we can't just assume the reader knows that that Peter's like what he's integrating nature with. And when we talk about the helping process, the help, helping who, what process, like we can't, these are, these are very ambiguous terms. I can't, if I'm, if I'm the reader, I can't sit back down and imagine what a helping process looks like. I have no, there's no metaphor for it. There's no, there is no thing in my mind that says, oh right, this is the helping process or the results, or if that impact the result, like then this is very ambiguous. And I love that saying a confused mind says no. So if I'm confused at all as the reader, I'm subconsciously saying no. The flip side of that is sometimes if we're a little ambiguous and we're going to explain later, then we can, we can keep attention by, by using that ambiguity, but that's not what's happening here. What's happening, I think is just, I think it's just a languaging issue that I suspect. I think it's probably a languaging issue and a familiarity issue. There's probably some, you know, English can be a very precise language in many ways, other languages have a little more room for metaphor and a little more ambiguity. I spent a couple of years learning Arabic and Arab and to be clear, not learning it well, but Arabic, it's very flower, it can have very flowery language and language with metaphor and it's not precise, whereas English might be more precise. English can be, you know, certainly have its own poetry as well, but when somebody is reading this in English, we want them to be able to, the clarity is critically important. So we, if we say this process or a helping process or these results, it'd be very clear what we're talking about because it's easy. If we make it, if we make it too much work, so if I read through these first five, six, seven paragraphs and I feel like I don't, I'm not really sure where, like, whoa, where am I here? I'm not, then it's easy to drift away and stop reading. But if I have a clear view and I know, like, okay, today's email is about how Peter Crier discovered a way to integrate, integrate nature into the, a therapeutic whatever, whatever, whatever the language would be around that or a hit, you know, integrate nature with his hit in the therapy practice. It's mentioned later. He's a hit in the therapist. So something that's very clear, what did he integrate nature into? What did, like, what did he do? And then ask yourself, why, why does the reader care? I mean, that's another, I'll sort of, I'll go down that road for a second, then I'll hand it off to you, Andre, because this is one to me that this is the nearest and dearest part to my heart. And from the editing perspective is I just ask over and over and over again, when I write, why does the reader care? Why, why, why, why? It's, it's, it's pathological at some point. So if you write a sentence, like, you know, tomorrow we will go into the powerful elements that nature provides for the helping process. Who cares? Why? Why do they care? Why does that matter? Right. And it sounds like I'm being combative with that's how I talk to myself. Because I eventually I get irritated by ants asking and answering asking and I've blurt something out in my mind, usually editing. And when I do that, I'm like, Oh, that's, that's the reason. And once I know the reason why somebody should care, then it helps me edit the overall writing to focus to always focus and refocus and bring the writing back to why it matters to the lead to the reader. Not what I'm trying to communicate. It's not what I say, it's what they hear, or it's not what I write, it's what they read. And having that frame of mind over and over is really, really important. And Cohen has done very well. I'm sort of conceptually with this where he's talking about I just saw this moment. Okay, this this paragraph says that's where we helping professionals get really excited. We want to grow. We want to learn. We want to help better the world. We want to improve ourselves so we contribute, contribute even more. Actually, I didn't intend to make this feedback, but I'll add this to listen to how how lyrical this is and then listen to where it breaks. So that's where we are. That's where we helping professionals get really excited. We want to grow. We want to learn. We want to help better the world. Right. The first three we set it up. We want to grow. We want to learn. We want to help. There's this lyrical nature. And then we we break it better the world. And it's another example. I don't know what that means. We want to grow. We want to learn. We want to help. And then the sentence better the world. Now I'm trying to figure out what does that mean. And then it shifts again. We want to improve ourselves so we can contribute even more. Like when you when you read it out loud, you'll hear it where it's lyrical. And then where we break the pattern. And sometimes that pattern break is it works, is it draws attention. And other times the pattern break, it feels like it's a little confusing. So, you know, I realize this sounds crazy. I do need to do this with every single paragraph, every single sentence. I'm going to say no, you don't have to. But the reality is yes, you should. You should just read your stuff out loud. Or have it, you can do the audio narration and listen to it or have somebody else read it to you. But you're but listen for those listen for the music in the writing. And do you know what do you know what it means? Like in that one paragraph, I know what some of it means, but I don't know what other parts of it mean. So, when he says that's where we helping professionals get really excited, I don't know what a helping professional is. It could be a firefighter, it could be a therapist. I don't know. Now I'm a little confused. What are what are helping professionals? So, be a little clearer about that. So, that's where we helping professionals get really excited. We want to grow, I get that. We want to learn, I understand that. We want to help. Yep, you're with me. Better the world. I don't know what that means. Are we talking about planting trees now? Or are we talking about helping people? Or like, I don't, now I'm confused. And then we switch back. We want to improve ourselves so we can contribute even more. Contribute to what? What am I contributing to? Right, that's this precision. And it sounds, if you're listening, you know, when you listen to this, you can think, wow, this guy's being a jackass. Like he's really getting detailed. But that's not, that's not the intent here. The intent is just to reflect back. What is the reader, what's going on in the reader's mind? What questions are he or she asking? And they're probably being asked subconsciously in microseconds. There's just, and it probably feels a little bit like dissonance. So if we, if someone reads the sentence, that's where we helping professionals get really excited. They might not stop and go, wait a minute, what the hell is the helping professional? But it might be this just little sense of, am I in the right place? Am I helping professionally? What, how is it? And then when you get farther along and we want to grow, I got now comfortable. We want to learn, I want to help. Yep, I do. Absolutely. Better the world. I don't know what that means. It's very subtle. But that's the, that's just part of the editing process. You know, is there anything in the writing that needs to be further specified? And when you find it, then you, that's where that's, it's just opportunity. It's opportunity to, to look at that paragraph and ask yourself, what am I trying to say in this paragraph? And what I think is being said in this, that paragraph is that, therapeutic professional, they're therapists, however, they would define the audience. They're, they're growing, they're learning, they're doing this to help, they're improving themselves so they can, they can improve the world. Right. That can be said very succinctly. So part of the editing process is to ask yourself, what was I trying to say in this paragraph? And could I say it better? And then you just rewrite it. Like that's, that's the magic. All right, I'll hand it off to you. Once I get talking about anything, you know. There's so many lessons in, in what you've just said. When I started working with, with my writing coach, well, as we mentioned in the past, one of the things that she, that, that I didn't really realize how bad I was at is exactly what you've just unpacked your ways. I will presume, because in my head, there's a narrative, I know these things. So the context, as I'm expressing it onto the page, I forget that other people aren't in my head. And even when I'm reading it off to the fact, yeah, I'm thinking to myself, this reads great. But from somebody else's perspective, it's, it's, it's this ambiguity. It's, well, it's, it, you know, there's, there's elements of it that just don't make a mistake. I mean, don't make sense because, or don't make as much sense as it should. So therefore, it's not as powerful as it should, because they don't have any context. So it's always important to, to help, to help them by defining these things, painting this picture explicitly when it, when need be, you know, you can, you can read a piece that's not your own. And even though it's well written, you can get to the end and think, like, you still don't know what it's about. Like what, what did I just read other than I enjoyed reading it? There's not much, and that's because of things like this, like that sentence, Sean, Sean highlighted is, there's a lot of ambiguity and it doesn't paint this vivid picture. You know, as you're reading through any email, any piece, any, anything that's, that's, that's written is our movie is in our head. You know, we're not watching a movie on the screen, whereas everything's completely different. So if you're not helping them paint that paint the picture you want to paint in their mind, then, then the writing is, is, is failing on a fundamental level. So, you know, these are, these are lessons that anybody needs to take in, including ourselves, because it's, it's easy just to miss these things. So one of the hardest work that it's your own. Yeah. That is just miserable to edit your own work. Yeah. The writer pisses you off because it's you. And you miss so you, there's so much to miss in your own writing. Yeah. Sorry, I think you've interrupted. You know, how my feelings about editing? Yeah, I mean, it's, it's, it's so much easier when there's two people. So, you know, when I'm writing something and then Sean's reading it, he gets to see all the stuff and, and vice versa. And if you're working with somebody else that's, that's reviewing slash editing stuff for like a beta reader, then, then they can catch these things. But when it's just you and yourself, and you're trying to, and you're trying to figure this out. Yeah, it can be, it can be challenging. So one way is to just listen to it out loud. But again, when the context and the, and all the nuances are in your head, it still can be challenging. So there's no easy, easy fix. So the piece that I want to highlight next again, this is, this is, I think, broadly useful for everyone. But in the context that Cohen has, has written it here, it's on the first page as I'm, as I'm looking at it, and it's, he's got it in, in brackets. And it says, so, so I'm quoting that. Who is this guy or better yet? Who is this very, very, the second very capitalized experience while educated and while 60 old therapist, coach, and teacher? So this is one of the cases and it, and it goes on a bit about that. And I think this is one of the cases where it's easy to be too explicit sometimes. And certain things can be more powerful when they're inferred. So, you know, this is similar to the, to the idea of showing versus telling. So instead of being explicit about the fact that he is very, very experienced, well, demonstrate his experience through, and it's done later on in the piece. So, which then makes this whole sentence redundant if it was just reworked further up. So you don't have to say somebody is very experienced. If I think it's further down here, it's mentioned that he's helped more than 10,000 students. So you, that just needs to be worked in further up that this guy, Peter, who has helped 10,000 students, he's been doing this for 60 years or I think he's 60 years old, but he's been doing this for, however long he's been doing this for. And then you can, then the reader's going to infer that, oh my God, this guy's very experienced. And I need to pay attention. And so that was that piece. And then the other nitpicky thing I'm going to draw on. Sometimes, let me see if I can find it quickly. It's, there's a piece here where he goes back in time. So he says, let's go back in time. I mean, he's, he's explicit about that. Can we do a find it? There we go. So, so this is at the end of page one of email one. And then it's let's go back in time. And then there's a few dots. And then, and then the very next sentences, because this journey started 40 years ago, which straight away, you don't need to say, let's go back in time, because if you, if the writing is, is clear enough that we've now gone back 40 years, that let's go back in time isn't, isn't necessary. And then again, when you're coming back to present time, if you just make it clear, you know, so nowadays or, you know, but it's, it's where it's clear that you've now left the 40 years ago. I know that's, this is just a nitpicky thing, but I think it's, it's all these little things that can work to make the copy, or to make the, the story tighter and, you know, just, just read easier instead of it being a little bit jarring. Let me see. Do you have anything on that? Yeah, it's, I have a similar reaction. And I was thinking about how to, how to explain this in a way that everybody can benefit from. And I can't take credit for this because it was, it was explained to me by a friend of mine, who's a phenomenal writer, David Deutsch. David had mentioned several times why he thinks it's important to copyright. If you're not familiar with the name David Deutsch, he's a A-list copywriter, world-class copywriter. And David was talking about why he thinks screenwriting is so important as a sort of a skill to develop. And, you know, it's one of those things that intellectually, I'm sure I was nodding my head like, oh yes, of course, screenwriting. But when, as soon as I, and I realized very quickly, I didn't know why it was, he thought it was so important. So I started to ask some questions and David, and his, David is just one of the nicest people in the world. And he's sort of, he's sent a very long email to be explained and demonstrated. But the difference, it's so profound. If you, as writers, we often describe something. So in Andre, you just gave a good example of this, and I'll just steal your example. So when, when Pete, when Cohen writes, back then he was one of my Dutch teachers, thankful to this day I met him and learned from his wisdom. So who is this guy? Or Betty, who is this very, very experienced, well-educated and last 60-plus-year-old therapist, coach, and teacher. Well, we're just, that's description. We're describing things. But screenwriting tells us to do instead, is to craft the scene where we see that he's very experienced, where we learn that he's well-educated and we, and we can infer that he is wise and a teacher. So to do that, we can mention, you know, we can mention things like, you know, when he started doing this 40 years ago, well, that tells us he's been at it a long time. That actually demonstrates experience. All of the things he's done demonstrate his experience. When we mentioned that he has taught 10,000 students later, that also, that's, that's, that's screenwriting. That's showing us a scene where he is teaching some, the loss of students or where he is having an epiphany a long time ago that set him on the path. Like that's, that's what we want our writing to do is to show us, show us a character who is doing things, not describe a sort of cardboard cut out and tell us about that person. Because if you think about it from, if someone has any resistance to your message at all, they can, they can push against statements like, this person is very, very experienced. And someone can say, well, wait, you know, maybe not. Maybe, you know, maybe I don't agree. Oh, they're well educated. Well, where do they go to school? Yeah, they might not think they're well educated. And, you know, are they wise? And we can, we're giving people things to push against and to subtly disagree with instead of just, just laying out the scene. And in the night, there's a paragraph here. That's another example of how to do this. So this, this is the paragraph. Peter lives in the most beautiful natural area of the Netherlands. I'm not going to pronounce what it's called. And then well known for its beautiful, diverse nature, full of wildlife and endless kilometers of purple hay in August. That, that paragraph is actually, it's a demonstration of both ways to write. And one of them is probably not necessary. The first one is a, it's sort of, it's, it's, it's a, it's an intellectualizing almost description, right? Peter lives in the most beautiful, natural area of the Netherlands. And then the next, you know, the next sentence describes natural beauty. You know, well known for its diverse, beautiful, diverse nature, full of wildlife, endless kilometers of purple hay in August. That, when we start talking about where Peter lives, don't tell us it's the most beautiful, natural area of the Netherlands. Tell us about the wildlife. Tell us about the endless kilometers of purple hay. Describe what this area looks like. And then we will reach the conclusion that this is a beautiful area of the Netherlands. Like that's, that's, this is, this is subtle, but it's not. Like our writing changes dramatically when we stop making a point and then, and then substantiating the point. And we just get better at describing what reality looks like that, that expresses the point. That is a very hard thing to do. It's something I struggle with every time I write. I describe things when I would be a much better writer if I could set scenes instead. So there's a lot of that. There's just, there's a lot of that writing in here and everybody does this. Everybody's emails that I read. There's, there's so much description of things rather than, than describing the scene where the scene tells us the things that we're trying to communicate. It lets, it makes the reader an active participant. That's one of the reasons we love fiction so much is that we have to create, if we're reading Harry Potter, our minds have to create the world in which Harry Potter lives. Yes, the written word is giving us the information, but our minds are making it. They're, they're, we're crafting that reality. It's not so overly descriptive. And when we're writing emails and bringing people on a journey, we want to, we want to have more of that. We want to have more of the reader actively engaged in painting a picture, imagining what the, the fields of purple hay look like. I can imagine that. I can't imagine if, if just looking at this paragraph again, if I just read this one sentence, Peter lives in the most beautiful natural area of the Netherlands. I don't, I, I have no image for that in my mind right now. It's, my mind's blank and then well known for its beautiful diverse nature, still no image full of wildlife, kind of an image, but I know if we're talking about, you know, deer or rhinos at this point, I'm guessing deer, then endless kilometers of purple hay in August, that I can see. I know what that is. Endless fields of purple hay. Okay. Now I have an image. So to, to re-craft that paragraph, I would re-craft it around rather than saying what the thing is, describe the thing so that I can then infer what it is. That's sort of the side of the tangent. Thanks for planning along. All right. I think it's smart now. No, I think, you know, this is, this is, this is great advice for people that have, you know, well, for all of us who have written our first shitty draft and, you know, our first drafts are never going to be, they're going to be anything but perfect. And there's going to be many, many times where we using exposition or we just describing something where we should be painting a picture. But once we go back the second and third time round, it's fairly easy at that point. Like we've, Sean's just identified that those few sentences, so it's a, so it's a single short paragraph. Well, you just highlight that or what I normally do is I'll hit, hit enter a few times. So I'll create some, some white space after white space with, it's the OCD in my mind. And then I'll try and rewrite that little scene in a way that's, that's, that's more visual. And then once I think I've got it right, I'll delete the sentence that I've just written. And, you know, now I've just improved everything by making that, that change. Yeah. And if you're doing this for somebody else, the nice thing with the Google Docs, as everybody knows, it's got, you just highlight like a whole sentence. So I've highlighted that, that whole paragraph. And then you can just click on the little plus sign and say add comment. And then you can just make that note for somebody else if you're doing editing for them, or they reading your thing. That's, that's a nice way of doing it. And then you can just rewrite those, those passages, because it's not always too, it's not always easy to, to catch the stuff yourself. Yeah. There's a couple of things that I'll draw attention to as well, just before I forget. The end of, there's a good, there's some interesting examples here of some things that Cohen's doing really well too. And there's a couple of picky things. But at the end of the first email, Cohen does a very, he does a great job opening interest in future emails. Yeah, he says at the end of the email. And then he says more about that in the last email, tomorrow we'll go in depth on what happens when nature is invited to helping professions into those powerful natural elements that you can use to see tomorrow. I suspect that's a, that's an English Dutch thing. So I would, I would change that to say more about that in the third email. So now I'm looking farther forward, or more about that later, however you want to do that. But this is a nice closing and then bringing me, and then in the beginning of the second email, he starts off by orienting. Yesterday was about Peter finding a very powerful way to use nature and all of its benefits as helping professional, even develop, even develop the method and training program about that. So it's, and then today we dive into what actually happens when we start using nature and what's possible out there. So that's a really, that's a great example of how we, we've had a conversation the day before, we've wrapped it up pointing to the future. And then we start the second day and we can, we let people know where they are in the process. And then what's, what's coming next? Cause they don't remember what you said yesterday. I was like, they made, if they're reading it, they may remember that there's some poll, they may feel some poll that they want to read it, but they didn't, I doubt that they, no one made a note, like a read tomorrow email to find out what happens to Peter. That's, that's more of this, this we're pulled toward it. So we're reminding them. Cohen does this very well in the beginning, he reminds them why, like what's going to happen today that yesterday they were interested in really is what he's doing. And another, just not to get too far down the weeds on this kind of stuff, but here's another interesting example of this of redundancy. They're redundant. There's so much redundancy in writing in general. We say the same thing two or three different ways. And then when we edit, it's really about saying, saying the thing once or saying it in a way that's clear and concise. So here, here's a section and there's a photo of this. So we put all of this together. This can be very powerful if it gets cleaned up. So he says, it's really amazing what happens because nature itself becomes part of the process. And therefore a CCE triangle arises CCE as in coach client environment. And then there's a picture that has the triangle with CCE on it. And then below it working indoors, there's only interaction between the professional and the client. This can be much tighter. So it is very, this is just, this is a broad lesson for everything in the writing. These two sentences and therefore a CCE triangle arises. I have no idea what the hell that means as the reader, no clue. And Cohen recognizes that and then explains it to me CCE as in coach client environment, combine these and just change those two sentences to and therefore a coach client environment triangle arises. It's less than half the length. And then you show the picture and then he explains working indoors, there's only interaction between the professional and the client. I would change that to the coach and the client. So now I know CCE and then when you get outside, we're adding this third element. Now I get it. It's just it's more impactful. And this is one of the things that you learn in editing. Fewer words means more impact per words. So if I say and therefore a CCE triangle arises CCE is a coach client environment, that is far less about less powerful than simply saying and therefore a coach client environment arises. Much clearer. Gotcha. And then now you're pulling me along forward. So editing, everything is editing. Everything is we write to get the ideas on the page. And then the editing is where all of the magic happens. And it's the hardest, unfortunately. Yeah, I like that. I like the the the image that Cohen has used. And it's fairly high up in email to it's near the top, but it's not at the top because there's some context that needed to be presented before the picture. But but the picture is still high up. You can probably see it at the near the top of the email. So I like that that combination. And as Sean's just demonstrated, if if this is tightened up, it'll move that image up even further. And I think that could be such a nice powerful combination to really hook somebody in. And although the the triangle has been superimposed on top. I think it still works really well to to draw in the tension. And that's that's been described as this coach client environment. And it's something that you can then you know, that that visual imagery is going to linger with you for the rest of the series. Every single time you you you hear coach client environment or the or the CC, the CCE triangle, that image is going to pop into your head. I like that. Yeah, there's just there's a there's a lot in here that's really good. There's a lot. And many of the emails are right. There's just there's a lot in there. It's just it's just it's good. It's good writing. It's interesting. It's fun to read. So these are the comments we're making. These are the these will improve something only because the something has been done. I want to there's a I want to give to this the second email to the end of the second email. There are two things I want to draw attention to. Just there's an interesting I had a couple of interesting lessons here. One of them is just the lyricism again, like listening to things. So these are these are two paragraphs. He says, well, walking, he got insights about camping, about buying a motor home, about working less than a few years stopping entirely. Okay, like that's when I read down, yeah, okay. But if you think about just a very subtle change, while working, he got insights about camping, about buying a motor home, about working less. It changes it. It's a it and when we read it, it's we look at it like, well, wait a minute, you know, working less than a few years stopping entirely. Why like, why does that matter? These are the subtle things, you know, we know what camping looks like. It's about camping, I can imagine camping, about buying a motor home, about working less. Yeah. But the and at the end of that third one, it interrupts the rhythm a little bit. It's and we rhythm is what pulls us in that lyricism, that's what pulls us forward. So when we when we look at our writing, sometimes we can see it, especially bolting and is a really good indicator. If we bolt on an and in another sentence, especially if we haven't done that before, that's a great opportunity to pause. But that this is just a way to think about your editing that punctuating part of it, where there's a musicality of it, where you're listening to the words and feeling that that lyricism is important. And then a second thing at the end. This is my this is a personal preference of mine. I'll share it. And then just some it'll resonate with some of you and some of you will think, I'm not sure this isn't this is not a right answer. This is just the way I think about it. So here's the end of email number two, last few paragraphs. For me, that wooden fence unleash unleashing those patterns and insights and the possibility to break through is true nature's magic. Can you imagine how this would be for you and your clients taking them outside? That's it for today. Tomorrow we will discover about synchronicities and how reading about mental health issues accelerated the adventure. Peter, see you tomorrow. In the what I just described the phrase that's it for today makes me want to pull my hair out. And I'll tell it to you from my perspective and then do with it what you want. And Audrey may have an entirely different view about this. I think we we tend to approach this a little differently. For me, when you say that's it for today, you just undermine everything that you've done today. And what today's email is is very good. It's good writing. There's a good story. It's very interesting. You're you're you're bringing it to a crescendo. I love this question. Can you imagine how this would be for you and your clients taking them outside? I want to imagine that and you're like, that's it for today. And it's like, wait, wait a minute. What? Don't it just it doesn't it doesn't need to be there. It's almost like this. It's your it's a subtle apology. Like you have, you know, that's it for today. Like it's and I get it. It's it's I may be inferring meaning but that to me, it's weird at the end. And to me, it's so much more powerful to say, you know, for me that wooden fence unleashing those patterns and insights and the possibility to break through is true nature's magic. Can you imagine how this would be for you and your clients taking them outside? Tomorrow, they'll discover about synchronicities and how reading about mental health issues accelerated the adventure with Peter. See you tomorrow. No apology needed. And I can't help myself. This the sentence. Can you imagine how this would be for you and your clients taking them outside? Right? This is a perfect example of why editing is important. Can you imagine how this would be for your clients? Like don't know what this is. And co-incenses that he that he hasn't explained what this is. And then he tells them what it is. Can you imagine how this would be for you and your clients taking them outside? That sentence should read. Can you imagine how taking your your taking your clients outside will and then whatever the benefit is, like tighten up that sentence. We say this or that. And then we explain what those things are. That's sloppy writing. We need to take the whatever it is we're explaining and put it in the sentence and make the sentence about that. So if there's if there's possibility of nature's magic, I want to know, can you imagine taking your clients out taking your clients outside and having them experience this magic? That's what you're asking. That's the clear sentence. That's what we want somebody. Or can you imagine the benefits of taking your clients outside? Can you imagine the transformative power of taking your clients outside? Like that's the sentence. Don't make the weird interrupt. Can you imagine what this would be for your clients? And now let me explain what this is. That's just that's not how we communicate to each other in the real world. We speak with power. So right with the same power. Alright. Say something smart, Andre. Yeah, I love that. Yeah. So I just want to share my experiences around what Sean's just articulated on how to potentially get get better at this. And unfortunately, it's not an easy answer. You know, getting better at this isn't easy. It requires practice. But to get there, this isn't about finding a coach because the coach is just going to highlight where you've made mistakes. But it's not going to, you know, there's there's another way that's going to, you just automatically going to get better over time, which is to read more. So I think one of the one of the most powerful things for me is is is seeking out that authors specifically fiction, authors that their writing resonates with me in a powerful way. And it's like, I want to not write like them because, you know, that's their style and my style is naturally just going to be different. But if I want to, if I want to look at an expression of somebody's writing that resonates with me, that I want to create a version of that for myself, and at least move in a direction of improving towards something is just seeking out authors that when you read their stories, it's just like outside of the actual story itself, the words, you know, move you, you know, the way that they've written a sentence, painted a picture, the lyrical nature of the prose, you know, every story and every author and every way that a story is written is going to is going to resonate differently with people. So, you know, for me, it's no surprise. Lee Child is my favorite author. I've been talking about it in my various courses for over a decade now. So Lee Child for me personally is I love his writing style. So I read everything he writes. And unfortunately, I've got to wait wait a year now. Each time he releases releases a new Jack Reacher book. But then there's other people like Elmore Leonard, who's got that same punchy style that breaks all sorts of rules. But it's it's the similar style to Lee Child. And for me, it's it's a similar style to who to who to how I want to write. So, you know, we all going to find different things. There's this, there's a beautiful story. Sean's Reddit, I haven't I haven't finished it yet, called The Overstory by Richard Powers. And, you know, reading things like that is going to transform how you write because in your head, you're just going to get better at at writing, bar reading, and then just enjoying, you know, you know, one of the things you could do is when you're reading as you read. So you initially reading as a reader, but then you also read as a writer. It's like, how is the sentence constructed? Like, you know, there's certain sentences are just going to stop you in your tracks. And then you're going to think to yourself, okay, what's just happened? How has he done this? Or how has she done this? So what I do is because I read most of my books on my Kindle, is I'll just highlight a passage and then you can say add note. And then I'll just tap in some some ideas that, you know, there's something about the sentence that that I like, I'll put some notes inside there. So then later, I can extract all those all those notes and highlights that I can analyze or just keep later on. So again, this isn't something that's a quick fix. If you're going to read fiction, it's not about reading one book and thinking, okay, job done. I'm done now for the rest of the year. It's it's about it's about reading a lot daily, if possible. So yeah, I think that's that's the last that's the last word I'll say. And I'll give us a slight counterpoint to that, but the same vein and the only the only difference being what to read, I think it benefits everyone to read journalism. And you don't and you don't have to go, you know, you don't have to read it in depth. But if you under and for me this, and I think this is very close to my heart, because I was an academic writer, I left graduate school and I went to work for a magazine in my first, and I thought, and based on my articles published in different journals when I was in graduate school, like co-wrote work was one of my professors, I felt like I was a decent writer and had certainly had that feedback a lot as a graduate student, as an undergraduate student, that was a good writer. And then the first article that I wrote where the magazine I worked for was destroyed. And the next article, I mean, there was more red ink than anything else. And the, and what I very quickly learned was that journalists get to the point very quickly. And there's this idea in journalism called burying the lead, which is, you don't, you don't put the main part of the story five paragraphs down. The lead is in the first paragraph, and journalists get very good, good journalists get their writing is very clear. You know, you're setting people up what to expect. And then, and you're delivering on it. And of course, there's a relationship between headlines and other things. But I would certainly recommend, go look at the Washington Post, go look at the New York Times, the Los Angeles Times, the larger newspapers, look at the headline, look at the first few paragraphs, they're very punchy. The New Yorker is another excellent example of good writing. I mean, I just opened up the New Yorkers were talking, and it's clear, right? So this is one of the articles. How did a linguist come to own an indigenous language? Okay, this, I know what that article is about. It's not, I'm not wondering, why is Russia falling behind on vaccinations? These are very clear. And if I go into these and into these articles, it when I, it's, it's, so this is Russia beat the world to a vaccine. So why is it falling behind on vaccinations? The problem that seems as well supply and demand. Now, I'm set up to understand what's coming next. And in the first paragraph, I'm off and running. So we're not writing, it's somewhere, a writing style exists somewhere between these, these different polls. So in Andre, and I exemplify this, that I, I write like a journalist, Andre writes much more like a fiction writer. And when we, we come to, and what I mean by that is there's, there, he has much more texture in his writing. There's, there's, there's much more of, of, there's, I'm struggling for the word, because there's just more to the writing. It's much more nuanced. It's a much richer writing than my, the way that I write, I write, I really write, like, trying to imagine when I'm writing with a slight chamber, I want my words to be powerful, I want my points to be clear. And when I make a point, I want it to be clear that I, how I've supported that point. But there's, there's far less texture in my writing. And when we, I think we bring these two worlds together. And when you do that, you get like a Michael Lewis, that's one of the reasons Michael Lewis is such a great writers. He has the, he has the eye of a narrative writer, he can write good stories, and he finds great characters, and you can fall in love with the characters as he writes them. But there, but he also is a powerful, powerful writer. He is taking you someplace, and he knows exactly where he's taking you, and he gets you there. And you always know where you are, and you, you always know he's taking you somewhere. But he's taking you there in the vehicle of really good characters, and very compelling stories that are not the typical story. And that, I mean, to me, that's the gold standard to me. And he's a profoundly good writer. So to have that target to reach, and each of you listening to this, and you need to find your style. Some of you will naturally gravitate to a more hard hitting, in your markets may reward more of a hard hitting journalistic style. Others might, might benefit from the lyricism of fiction and more story, or you might find that there's a little bit of both and bring them together. And there's the, the structure of the journalistic journey, where you know, you're, there's a point you're making, and you're taking somebody someplace, but that you're bringing the skills of a fiction writer of a story, the narration, screenwriting, and as you're bringing that into the writing, and then just plan on spending decades learning how to do this. That's all. I mean, in Andre, I've written a lot, you know, millions of words, and we still, I mean, we are still right up against our abilities every single day. And knowing that we will die long before we can ever get nearly as good as we want to. It's that idea from Elizabeth Gilbert that I know for me, my abilities don't match my tastes. I read my writing, I think. Damn it. There's so much more, so much more to learn. All right, anything to, I think it's a bit about an hour today. I'm going to wrap up with anything else. And Cohen, I'll, I'll say my last piece and then hand it off to you to close. But, you know, Cohen, great work. And it was, it was a wonderful experience. So congratulations to you, everybody who's done the work. Congratulations. So take the feedback from the perspective of that. You, you did something, you accomplished something very important here. Congratulations. Yeah, Ditto. This, this piece stood out and which is why we wanted to just use it as an example we thought would be broadly useful for everyone. I think one of the things I'll do that, that we haven't done with, with the previous reviews is, is I'll include maybe four, I'll include, as I'm thinking out now, thinking out loud, four links to four articles, one, which will be one of the two that, that Sean just mentioned, one of those journalistic ones that he's just written, that he just mentioned about Russia or the one before that. So we'll, we'll link out to one of those, which is, which is going to be an authorial narration. So those sorts of pieces, there isn't, there isn't any opinion. It's, it's, it's very fact based. And that's obviously, it's good to see how process those, those articles are written and learn from that. But at the same time, is that's not how we're writing our landing pages and our emails is because it's, it's, it's missing that the narrative piece of it, you know, the, well, the, the storytelling component. And there's a, there's a piece I've read years ago from George Sanders. George Sanders is a, is a professor of writing at one of the universities and, and an author, he publishes fiction and nonfiction, but he's, he's, he's got a lovely, he wrote an article for the, for the Guardian a few years ago on, on how he writes. So it'll be useful to read it because he, he explains how he writes and how he approaches writing. So it's a, it's a nonfiction piece, but there is so much personality in it because it's him talking about his journey, you know, so it's, so it's first person. So you can contrast that to, to a more journalistic piece that's, that's more fact, facts based. And then we'll include one from a Michael Lewis, because Sean is completely correct. You know, Michael Lewis has nailed that, that perfect balance of somebody that can, that can weave storytelling into something that can be really boring and dry, you know, some, some of the, the subjects that his books are based around is like, there's no one on earth that can make this sound amazing yet he does. And then maybe one of Malcolm Gladwell's nonfiction pieces, because he's, he's also really good. But again, those, those, those four pieces that we all share will create a very, you know, there'll be a contrast between those things and some way between that, you'll find something that resonates with you in how you structure your, your landing pages, the emails you write, everything you do. There'll be lessons just by reading those pieces and then just figuring out where you want to be and where you want to land, and then dive in deeper there. So that's all I'm going to say. Good work, everyone. Doing my best not to make any comments about Malcolm Gladwell when you were in that door. This is me like sweating bullets over here, not trying to say anything, but just because I opened the door not to have an open loop, Malcolm Gladwell is an outstanding writer. However, this, the idea that he popularized the 10,000 hour rule, it makes me, this is, I think this is a really interesting example of where writers can go awry. So the, at nope, his, his description of the 10,000 hour rule is wrong. It is not how the author, Kaye enters Erickson. It's not at all what the research said and Malcolm Gladwell never discussed it with the authors of the research to understand it. So this is a great example of really good writing and a really good writer who like all of us can have some blind spots. And for Malcolm Gladwell as an extraordinary ever writer as he is, he too has blind spots. He too has this, he has, he has done something in his writing that really is, is just not, it's, it's indefensible. Yet he's an incredible writer. And I mentioned that for two reasons. One, because it drives me crazy that he doesn't get more attention for having made that mistake because he's never corrected it. But also because the point out for all of us, we all have blind spots. The writers you think about it being incredible writers, they are still, they are hammering away. It's like Hemingway, Hemingway said that, you know, writing is easy. You just sit down and type writer and bleed. Sometimes writing feels like that. So don't, when we describe, and when we edit, and we're doing these reviews, it's easy to hear that and think, well, they've figured some of this out. We haven't figured out anything. These are just like all of you. It's just, you just go in and you do it and you write a little bit better tomorrow. Sometimes you write a little worse. You know, it is, but don't, don't think about writing as being something that you're going to learn how to do and check a box like now I can write. It's, it's, if our experience is any indicator, that's not what's going to happen. You're just going to write and write and write. And there will be days when you dislike your writing less than other days. So that's, I'll leave it at that. All right. Thanks everybody. Take care everyone.
